front: Poem: a ship strikes against 'unbelievable Floridas' after its haulers are killed by 'gaudy redskins' who nail them to stakes
Poem: the narrator ship yearns for 'Europe with its ancient parapets' and declares 'the Dawns are heartbreaking'
Poem: the ship is described as 'lighter than a cork' and 'swollen with intoxicating torpor'
Poem: the narrator has seen 'the low-hanging sun speckled with mystic horrors' and 'fish-traps where a whole Leviathan rots in the rushes'
Poem: the narrator calls upon 'million golden birds, Life Force of the future' and sees 'singing phosphorous'
Poem: heavily inspired by Jules Verne's Twenty Thousand Leagues Under the Sea; sent to Paul Verlaine when the author was 16
Poem: a 'cold puddle' where a 'squatting child' releases a boat 'fragile as a May butterfly'
Poem: opens 'Once, if I remember correctly, my life was a banquet where every heart revealed itself'
Poem: contains two 'Delirium' sections -- 'Foolish Virgin' (representing Verlaine) and 'Alchemy of the Word'
Poem: in 'Alchemy of the Word,' the speaker declares 'I invented the colour of vowels!' and lists 'Black A, white E, red I, blue O, green U'
Poem: the speaker placed 'Beauty on my knee' and found her 'bitter'
Poem: the final 'Farewell' section declares 'One must be absolutely modern'; ends with 'This is the time of the Assassins'
Poem: contains the section 'Bad Blood'; the speaker calls himself 'a black' and recalls how his barbaric ancestors buttered their hair
Poem: assigns colors to the five vowels: 'A black, E white, I red, U green, O blue'
Poem: A is a 'black velvety jacket of brilliant flies which buzz around cruel smells'; O is the 'Omega, violet ray of [his] eyes'
Poem: O is a 'supreme Clarion full of strange piercing sounds' with 'silences crossed by Worlds and by Angels'
Work: opens with 'After the Flood,' in which a hare 'said its prayers to the rainbow through the spider's web'; includes 'Being Beauteous'
Work: John Ashbery translated this collection in 2011, claiming 'we are absolutely modern' because the author 'commanded us to be'
Work: letters to Georges Izambard and Paul Demeny declaring 'I is another' and advocating a 'long, immense, and deliberate derangement of the senses'
Work: the author states 'the poet is truly the thief of fire' in these letters
Poem: an 'open-mouthed, bare-headed' soldier 'sleeping' in a 'green hole where a river sings' is revealed to have 'two red holes' in his side
back: The Sleeper in the Valley (Arthur Rimbaud)

front: Author: gave up writing poetry at age 19-21 and became a gunrunner in Ethiopia and East Africa
Author: Verlaine included him in the 1884 anthology Les Poetes maudits and depicted him as an evil angel in 'Crime of Love'
Author: Henry Miller wrote a study of him titled The Time of the Assassins
Author: Patti Smith described her 'unrequited love' for this poet in Just Kids
